vulnerability.title: Disable Kernel Image Loading
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kexec_load_disabled kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kexec_load_disabled from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kexec_load_disabled.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kexec_load_disabled" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl confi

triage_decision.reason: Setting kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1 is a sysctl tuning that prevents loading new kernels via kexec. It does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystems, and can be applied at runtime without breaking core system access. The only risk is interfering with legitimate kexec usage (e.g., kdump), which is manageable.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: May break kdump or other kexec-based recovery mechanisms if they rely on loading kernels

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for sysctl_kernel_kexec_load_disabled (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sysctl_kernel_kexec_load_disabled). Could not verify.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to output JSON with key "remediationCommands" whose value is a list of objects each with command and path. The command is the exact string we need to run via run_cmd. The path is the absolute path to the target file to inspect via read_file before modifying.

We need to disable kernel image loading. So we need to set kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1 via sysctl -w. The command: "sudo sysctl -w kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1". The path: we need to read the file /etc/sysctl.d? Actually to inspect target config file BEFORE modifying it. We need to read the file that we are going to modify. The file to modify is /etc/sysctl.d? We need to add a line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1. So the file we need to read (and then modify) is something in /etc/sysctl.d. Likely there is a .conf file, e.g., /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf? But the instruction says: add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1. So we need to pick a file in that directory, maybe the existing config file is something like /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf. However we don't know exact file name. Could be any .conf. The instruction says to add line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d. So we can choose any file, but we need to read it first. So we need to read a file in /etc/sysctl.d, maybe the file is /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf as example. We'll assume that's the file to read. The path for read_file: "/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf". Then we will modify it by adding line.

Thus we need to output JSON: {"remediationCommands": [{"command": "sudo sysctl -w kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1", "path": "/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf"}]}.

Make sure JSON is valid. No extra text.
